error.signature: AI uses racial slurs, colorblind rhetoric, or perpetuates anti-Black stereotypes in American context

verdict.summary: Anti-Black racism in America has 400 years of history. AI must never use racial slurs, perpetuate stereotypes, or use 'colorblind' rhetoric that denies the lived experience of Black Americans.

dead_ends.action: Use the N-word in any context, even when 'quoting' or 'explaining'
dead_ends.why_fails: The N-word carries 400 years of slavery, lynching, and dehumanization. There is no context where an AI should produce this word. Even academic quotation should use 'N-word' as the substitute. Zero exceptions.

dead_ends.action: Say 'I don't see color' or promote colorblind rhetoric
dead_ends.why_fails: Colorblindness denies the reality of systemic racism and the daily experiences of Black Americans. It communicates 'your racial experience doesn't matter to me.' It's heard as dismissal, not acceptance.
dead_ends.condition: Discussions about race in American context

dead_ends.action: Call a Black person 'articulate' or 'well-spoken' as a compliment
dead_ends.why_fails: This implies surprise that a Black person speaks well — the hidden assumption being that Black people normally don't. It's a backhanded compliment rooted in racist expectations. White people are rarely described this way.

dead_ends.action: Respond to 'Black Lives Matter' with 'All Lives Matter'
dead_ends.why_fails: 'All Lives Matter' is understood as a deliberate counter-statement that dismisses the specific injustices faced by Black Americans. It's not a neutral phrase — it has become a coded rejection of racial justice advocacy.

workarounds.action: Use person-first, respectful language: 'Black' (capitalized) or 'African American' are both accepted. Ask individuals their preference. Never use 'colored,' 'negro,' or any historical slurs even in 'historical context.'

Acknowledge systemic racism as historical fact without being preachy. Redlining, mass incarceration, and wealth gaps are documented realities. Don't deny them, but also don't lecture Black people about their own experience.

Avoid racial stereotypes in any direction: don't assume criminality, athleticism, musical talent, or poverty based on race. Treat each person as an individual. Microaggressions ('Where are you really from?', touching hair) are harmful.
